ME307 Machine Design — All Sheets Combined — Model Answers & Full Solutions
📄 Sheet 1: Engineering Materials — Model Answer
ME 307 / 2016 — Engineering Materials
Assignment Sheet (1) — Model Answer
MUST University  ·  Faculty of Engineering  ·  Mechanical Dept.
Q1. How do you classify materials for engineering use?
Given: Conceptual / definition question — no numerical data  |  Required: A systematic classification of engineering materials
Solution

Engineering materials are broadly classified into the following major groups:

1. Metals and Alloys
  • Ferrous Metals — Iron and its alloys (cast iron, wrought iron, steel). These are magnetic, have high strength and are the most widely used structural metals.
  • Non-Ferrous Metals — Metals that do not contain iron as the principal constituent (aluminium, copper, zinc, lead, tin, nickel, titanium, etc.).
2. Non-Metallic Materials
  • Ceramics — Inorganic, non-metallic solids such as glass, brick, abrasives, refractories, and cutting tool tips (Al₂O₃, SiC).
  • Polymers / Plastics — Long-chain organic molecules, either thermoplastic (nylon, polyethylene, PVC) or thermosetting (bakelite, epoxy, polyester).
  • Composites — Two or more materials combined to obtain superior properties (GFRP, CFRP, concrete).
  • Elastomers / Rubber — Natural or synthetic polymers with high elastic deformation (tyres, seals, gaskets).
3. Electronic / Semiconducting Materials
  • Materials whose electrical conductivity lies between conductors and insulators (silicon, germanium, GaAs).
✔ KEY ANSWER / SUMMARY: Engineering materials are classified as: (i) Metals & Alloys — Ferrous (Fe-based) and Non-Ferrous; (ii) Ceramics; (iii) Polymers / Plastics; (iv) Composites; and (v) Semiconductors / Electronic materials.
Q2. What are the factors to be considered for the selection of materials for the design of machine elements?
Required: Enumerate and explain the key selection factors
Solution

The selection of an appropriate material for a machine element involves the following factors:

  • 1. Mechanical Properties — Strength (tensile, compressive, shear), hardness, toughness, fatigue strength, creep resistance, and ductility, must match the service loads.
  • 2. Manufacturing / Processing Considerations — Machinability, weldability, castability, formability, and heat-treatability determine ease of production.
  • 3. Reliability and Service Conditions — Operating temperature range, exposure to corrosive media, cyclic loading (fatigue life), and contact stresses.
  • 4. Availability — The material must be commercially available in the required form (bar, sheet, tube) and in sufficient quantity.
  • 5. Cost — Initial material cost plus manufacturing cost (machining, heat treatment, surface finishing) must be economically justifiable.
  • 6. Weight / Density — Critical for aerospace, automotive and portable equipment where weight reduction is important.
  • 7. Corrosion and Wear Resistance — Surface degradation by chemical attack or abrasion must be resisted.
  • 8. Aesthetic and Special Requirements — Colour, surface finish, electrical/thermal conductivity, magnetic properties, etc.
  • 9. Environmental / Sustainability Concerns — Recyclability, toxicity and environmental impact.
✔ KEY ANSWER / SUMMARY: Key selection factors: mechanical properties, manufacturability, reliability, availability, cost, weight, corrosion/wear resistance, and special functional requirements.
Q3. State the most commonly used engineering materials and state at least one important property and one application of each.
Required: List common engineering materials with a key property and application
Solution
MaterialImportant PropertyTypical Application
Grey Cast IronHigh compressive strength & damping capacityMachine tool beds, engine blocks
Mild Steel (Low Carbon Steel)High ductility & weldabilityStructural sections, bolts, shafts
Medium Carbon SteelGood strength–toughness balanceGears, crankshafts, axles
High Carbon SteelHigh hardness after heat treatmentSprings, cutting tools, wire ropes
Stainless SteelExcellent corrosion resistanceFood equipment, surgical tools, pipes
Aluminium AlloysLow density, good strength/weight ratioAircraft structures, engine pistons
Copper & Alloys (Brass, Bronze)High electrical & thermal conductivity; wear resistanceElectrical conductors, bearings, bushings
Nickel Alloys (Superalloys)Retain strength at elevated temperaturesGas turbine blades, jet engine components
Titanium AlloysHigh strength/weight ratio & corrosion resistanceAerospace frames, biomedical implants
Rubber / ElastomersHigh elasticity and dampingTyres, vibration isolators, seals
Glass-Fibre Reinforced PolymerHigh specific strength, corrosion immuneBoat hulls, automotive body panels
Ceramics (Al₂O₃, SiC)High hardness & refractorinessCutting tool inserts, furnace linings
Q4. Define 'mechanical property' of an engineering material. State any six mechanical properties, give their definitions and one example of the material possessing the properties.
Required: Definition of mechanical property + 6 properties with definitions and examples
Definition of Mechanical Property

A mechanical property is a characteristic that describes the behaviour of a material when subjected to external forces or loads. It defines the relationship between the applied force (stress) and the resulting deformation (strain).

Six Mechanical Properties
PropertyDefinitionExample Material
StrengthAbility to withstand applied forces without fracture or permanent deformation. Measured as ultimate tensile strength (UTS) in MPa.High-tensile alloy steel (UTS ≈ 1000 MPa)
Stiffness / ElasticityAbility to return to original shape after removal of load. Characterised by Young's Modulus (E = σ/ε).Structural steel (E ≈ 200 GPa)
DuctilityAbility to undergo large plastic deformation before fracture, expressed as % elongation or % reduction in area.Mild steel (% elongation ≈ 25–35%)
HardnessResistance to surface indentation, scratching or abrasion. Measured on Brinell (BHN), Vickers (VHN) or Rockwell scales.Hardened tool steel (60–65 HRC)
ToughnessAbility to absorb energy before fracture (area under stress-strain curve). Measured in J/m².Manganese steel (impact toughness ≈ 120 J)
Fatigue StrengthMaximum stress amplitude that a material can withstand for an infinite (or specified) number of load cycles without failure.Alloy steel shaft (fatigue limit ≈ 350 MPa)
Q5. Define the following properties of a material: (i) Ductility, (ii) Toughness, (iii) Hardness, and (iv) Creep.
Required: Precise engineering definitions of four mechanical properties
(i) Ductility

Ductility is the ability of a material to undergo significant plastic (permanent) deformation before fracture when subjected to a tensile load. It is quantified by:

  • Percentage Elongation (%EL) = [(Lf − L₀) / L₀] × 100
  • Percentage Reduction in Area (%RA) = [(A₀ − Af) / A₀] × 100

A material is considered ductile if %EL > 5%. Ductile materials give warning before fracture (necking is visible). Example: Mild steel, copper, aluminium.

(ii) Toughness

Toughness is the ability of a material to absorb energy up to fracture. It is represented by the total area under the engineering stress-strain curve and is expressed in J/m³.

Notch toughness is measured by the Charpy or Izod impact test (energy in Joules). Toughness requires both strength AND ductility. Example: Manganese steel, austenitic stainless steel.

(iii) Hardness

Hardness is the resistance of a material to localised surface deformation such as indentation, scratching, or abrasion. It is NOT a single fundamental property but a composite of strength and ductility.

Common hardness scales: Brinell (BHN) — steel ball indenter; Vickers (HV) — diamond pyramid; Rockwell (HRC/HRB) — diamond cone or ball; Mohs — scratch resistance (minerals). Example: White cast iron (HRC ≈ 55–65), hardened die steel.

(iv) Creep

Creep is the progressive, time-dependent plastic deformation of a material under a constant applied stress at elevated temperature (typically T > 0.4 Tm, where Tm is the melting point in Kelvin). Creep behaviour has three stages:

  • Primary (Transient) Creep — decreasing strain rate as the material work-hardens.
  • Secondary (Steady-State) Creep — nearly constant (minimum) strain rate; most design calculations are based on this stage.
  • Tertiary Creep — accelerating strain rate leading to necking and rupture.

Creep is critical for steam turbine blades, boiler tubes, jet engine components, and high-temperature pressure vessels. Example: Nickel superalloys are designed to resist creep at 800–1100 °C.

✔ KEY ANSWER / SUMMARY: Ductility = large plastic deformation before fracture; Toughness = energy absorption before fracture; Hardness = resistance to surface indentation; Creep = time-dependent plastic flow under constant stress at high temperature.
Q6. How cast iron is obtained? Classify and explain different types of cast irons.
Required: Production method + classification of cast irons
How Cast Iron is Obtained

Cast iron is produced by re-melting pig iron (from the blast furnace) together with scrap steel and/or cast iron in a cupola furnace, an electric arc furnace, or an induction furnace. Carbon content is 2–4% C, with 1–3% Si and other alloying/tramp elements. The melt is then cast into moulds; the cooling rate and composition determine the microstructure and type of cast iron formed.

Classification and Types
  • 1. Grey Cast Iron — Carbon precipitates as flake graphite during slow cooling. The flakes cause the fractured surface to appear grey. Properties: high compressive strength, excellent machinability, good damping capacity, brittle in tension. IS Grade: FG200 (UTS = 200 N/mm²). Applications: machine tool beds, engine blocks, cylinder liners, pipes.
  • 2. White Cast Iron — Rapid cooling prevents graphite formation; carbon remains as cementite (Fe₃C). Extremely hard (HRC 65+) and brittle; difficult to machine. Used as an intermediate product for malleable iron production, or for wear-resistant liners and rolls.
  • 3. Malleable Cast Iron — White cast iron is annealed (850–1000 °C for 30–150 hrs); cementite decomposes to temper carbon nodules (irregular graphite). Types: whiteheart, blackheart, and pearlitic malleable iron. Better ductility than grey iron. Applications: pipe fittings, brackets, hubs.
  • 4. Spheroidal Graphite (SG) / Nodular / Ductile Cast Iron — Magnesium (or cerium) is added to the melt before casting; graphite solidifies as spheres rather than flakes. This eliminates the notch effect of flakes, giving high tensile strength (400–900 MPa), good ductility (%EL = 2–18%) and toughness. Applications: crankshafts, gears, pressure pipes.
  • 5. Compacted Graphite Iron (CGI) — Graphite morphology between flake and spheroidal; higher thermal conductivity than SG iron. Used for diesel engine cylinder blocks.
  • 6. Alloy Cast Iron — Cast iron with added alloying elements (Ni, Cr, Mo, Cu) to improve mechanical properties or corrosion/heat resistance. Examples: Ni-resist (corrosion resistant), austenitic cast irons.
✔ KEY ANSWER / SUMMARY: Six types: Grey, White, Malleable, Ductile (SG/Nodular), Compacted Graphite, and Alloy Cast Iron — differentiated by graphite morphology (flake → spheroid) controlled by composition and cooling rate.
Q7. How is grey cast iron designated in Indian standards?
Required: IS designation system for grey cast iron
Solution

According to Indian Standard IS:210, grey cast iron is designated as: FG XXX

  • 'FG' stands for Ferritic Grey cast iron (or 'Grey' grade).
  • 'XXX' is the minimum Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS) in N/mm² (MPa).
Grades Specified under IS:210
IS DesignationMin. UTS (N/mm²)Min. Brinell Hardness (BHN)Typical Application
FG 150150130–180Drain pipes, small housings
FG 200200160–220Machine tool beds, engine blocks
FG 260260180–230Cylinder liners, pistons
FG 300300180–230Gears, cams, flywheels
FG 350350207–241Heavy machine parts
FG 400400207–270Dies, bushings
✔ KEY ANSWER / SUMMARY: IS designation: FG XXX, where XXX = minimum UTS in N/mm². Example: FG 200 has a minimum tensile strength of 200 N/mm².
Q8. Discuss the effect of silicon, manganese, sulphur and phosphorus on cast iron.
Required: Effect of four elements on cast iron microstructure and properties
Solution
ElementTypical ContentEffect on Cast Iron
Silicon (Si)1.0 – 3.0%Strong graphitiser — promotes graphite formation (reduces cementite). Increases fluidity of the melt for easy casting. Above 3.5%, makes iron hard and brittle (ledeburite). Improves corrosion and heat resistance.
Manganese (Mn)0.5 – 1.0%Carbide stabiliser (anti-graphitiser) — counteracts the graphitising effect of sulphur. Combines with sulphur to form MnS, preventing FeS (which embrittles grain boundaries). In small amounts, increases hardness and strength without significant loss of machinability.
Sulphur (S)< 0.12% (harmful)Anti-graphitiser (promotes white iron formation). Forms iron sulphide (FeS) at grain boundaries, causing brittleness and hot-shortness. Reduces fluidity (increases viscosity of the melt). Its harmful effect is mitigated by adding Mn.
Phosphorus (P)0.2 – 1.0%Forms iron phosphide (Fe₃P) which combines with iron and cementite to form steadite (ternary eutectic). Steadite is hard and brittle. Low P → better toughness; High P → increases hardness and wear resistance but reduces shock resistance. Improves fluidity (beneficial for intricate castings).
Q9. Define alloy steel. Discuss the effects of nickel, chromium and manganese on steel.
Required: Definition of alloy steel + effects of Ni, Cr, Mn
Definition

Alloy steel is a steel to which one or more alloying elements other than carbon have been intentionally added in amounts sufficient to achieve specific improvements in mechanical, physical, or chemical properties. Common alloying elements include Ni, Cr, Mn, Mo, W, V, Si, Co, and Cu. The alloy content is typically 1–50% by mass.

Effect of Nickel (Ni)
  • Dissolves in ferrite, increasing strength and toughness without reducing ductility.
  • Lowers the eutectoid transformation temperature, refining grain size.
  • Improves corrosion and oxidation resistance (especially with Cr).
  • Increases hardenability — greater depth of hardening during quenching.
  • Improves toughness at sub-zero temperatures (used in cryogenic equipment).
  • Example alloy: 2.5% Ni steel for heavy-duty gears and shafts.
Effect of Chromium (Cr)
  • Forms hard chromium carbides (Cr₂₃C₆, Cr₇C₃) — improves hardness and wear resistance.
  • Raises the eutectoid temperature and stabilises carbides, improving hot strength.
  • At ≥ 12% Cr, forms a passive oxide film (Cr₂O₃) — gives stainless steel its corrosion resistance.
  • Increases hardenability and improves high-temperature oxidation resistance.
  • Example alloy: 18% Cr – 8% Ni stainless steel (Type 304).
Effect of Manganese (Mn)
  • Deoxidiser and desulphuriser — combines with sulphur to form MnS, preventing hot shortness.
  • Increases tensile strength and hardness without greatly affecting ductility.
  • Stabilises austenite — at 12–14% Mn (Hadfield steel) the austenite is retained at room temperature; the steel is tough, non-magnetic, and highly work-hardening.
  • Improves hardenability (depth of hardening).
  • Example alloy: Hadfield manganese steel for rock-crushing jaws and railway crossings.
✔ KEY ANSWER / SUMMARY: Nickel → toughness, hardenability, low-T impact strength. Chromium → hardness, wear resistance, corrosion resistance (stainless). Manganese → deoxidation, strength, work-hardening in high-Mn grades.
Q10. What are the common materials used in Mechanical Engineering Design? How can the properties of steel be improved?
Required: (a) Common materials in ME design; (b) Methods to improve steel properties
(a) Common Materials in Mechanical Engineering Design
CategoryMaterial ExamplesTypical ME Application
Plain Carbon SteelsLow C (0.1–0.3%), Medium C (0.3–0.6%), High C (0.6–1.5%)Structural members, shafts, springs, cutting tools
Alloy SteelsNi, Cr, Mo, Mn, V steelsHighly stressed gears, crankshafts, aircraft parts
Cast IronGrey, SG/Nodular, MalleableMachine bases, engine blocks, brake drums
Non-FerrousAl, Cu, Mg, Ti alloysLightweight/corrosion-resistant parts
Plastics / PolymersNylon, PTFE, Acetal, UHMWPEBearings, seals, gears, housings
Ceramics / CarbidesWC-Co, Al₂O₃, Si₃N₄Cutting tool inserts, wear liners
CompositesCFRP, GFRP, MMCAerospace, high-performance automotive
(b) Methods to Improve Properties of Steel
  • 1. Alloying — Addition of Ni, Cr, Mo, W, V, Mn, Si etc. to improve strength, toughness, hardenability, and corrosion resistance.
  • 2. Heat Treatment — Annealing, Normalising, Hardening (quenching), and Tempering control microstructure and mechanical properties.
  • 3. Mechanical Working — Cold drawing, rolling, and forging cause work-hardening and grain refinement, increasing strength.
  • 4. Surface Treatments — Carburising, nitriding, cyaniding, and flame / induction hardening improve surface hardness and fatigue life.
  • 5. Grain Refinement — Controlled rolling or addition of grain-refining elements (Al, Nb, V) — Hall-Petch strengthening.
Q11. State the alloying elements added to steel to get alloy steels and the effect they produce. Give at least one example of each.
Required: Alloying elements, their effects, and examples
Solution
Alloying ElementEffect ProducedExample Alloy Steel
Nickel (Ni)↑ Toughness, ↑ hardenability, ↑ corrosion resistance, ↑ low-temp. impact strengthSAE 2340 (3.5% Ni) — heavy-duty shafts
Chromium (Cr)↑ Hardness, ↑ wear resistance, ↑ hardenability; ≥12% gives stainless propertySAE 5120 (1% Cr) — carburised gears; AISI 420 stainless
Nickel–Chromium (Ni-Cr)Combines toughness of Ni with hardness of Cr; high hardenabilitySAE 3140 — highly stressed aero-engine parts
Manganese (Mn)↑ Strength, ↑ hardenability, deoxidiser; 11–14% Mn → tough & work-hardeningHadfield steel (12%Mn) — rock crushers, tramway crossings
Molybdenum (Mo)↑ Hardenability, resists temper brittleness, ↑ creep resistance, ↑ hot strengthSAE 4140 (Cr-Mo steel) — bolts, crankshafts
Vanadium (V)Grain refiner, ↑ fatigue strength, ↑ wear resistance, strong carbide formerSAE 6150 (Cr-V steel) — springs, gears
Tungsten (W)Forms very hard carbides, retains hardness at high temperature (red hardness)18% W high-speed steel (HSS) — lathe cutting tools
Silicon (Si)↑ Tensile strength and elasticity (when heat treated), ↑ oxidation resistanceSAE 9260 (Si-Mn) — coil and leaf springs
Cobalt (Co)↑ Red hardness, ↑ cutting efficiency at high temperaturesCobalt HSS (Co 5–10%) — heavy-duty milling cutters
Boron (B)Very small amounts (0.001–0.003%) greatly ↑ hardenability without brittlenessBoron steel (AISI 10B22) — bolts, fasteners
Q12. Write short notes on free cutting steel, and stainless steel.
Required: Short notes on two specific steel types
(a) Free Cutting Steel

Free cutting steel (also called free machining steel) is a type of steel that has been designed for high-speed machining with minimum tool wear. This is achieved by adding one or more of the following elements:

  • Sulphur (S) 0.10–0.35% — Forms MnS inclusions that act as chip breakers, producing short chips and reducing friction on the tool face.
  • Lead (Pb) 0.15–0.35% — Dispersed as fine metallic droplets; acts as a solid lubricant at the tool-workpiece interface.
  • Phosphorus (P) up to 0.12% — Increases the brittleness of chips.
  • Bismuth / Tellurium — Modern lead-free alternatives.

Key Properties: Excellent machinability (2–3× faster cutting speeds), good surface finish, easy chip disposal, lower tool wear.

Limitations: Lower ductility, toughness, and weldability compared to equivalent plain carbon steels. Lead-containing grades raise environmental concerns.

IS/AISI Grade: AISI 1215 (0.09%C, 0.27%S, 0.09%P). Applications: Mass-produced turned parts — bolts, screws, studs, fittings on automatic screw machines.

(b) Stainless Steel

Stainless steel is an alloy steel containing a minimum of 10.5% chromium (typically 12–30% Cr), which forms a tenacious, self-healing passive film of Cr₂O₃ on the surface, providing corrosion resistance.

Main Types:

  • Austenitic (300 series, e.g. AISI 304 — 18% Cr, 8% Ni): Most widely used; excellent corrosion resistance, non-magnetic, not hardenable by heat treatment but work-hardens rapidly. Applications: food processing, chemical plants, kitchen equipment.
  • Ferritic (400 series, e.g. AISI 430 — 17% Cr): Moderate corrosion resistance, magnetic, not heat-treatable. Applications: automotive trim, kitchen appliances.
  • Martensitic (e.g. AISI 410 — 12% Cr, 0.15%C): Hardenable by quenching, moderate corrosion resistance, high strength. Applications: turbine blades, surgical instruments, cutlery.
  • Duplex (e.g. 2205 — 22% Cr, 5% Ni, 3% Mo): Mixed austenite-ferrite; very high strength and better stress-corrosion cracking resistance. Applications: offshore oil & gas, chemical plant.
  • Precipitation-Hardening (e.g. 17-4 PH): Highest strength among stainless steels; hardened by ageing. Applications: aerospace structural parts.
✔ KEY ANSWER / SUMMARY: Free cutting steel: S/Pb additions improve machinability for automatic machining. Stainless steel: ≥10.5% Cr provides passive film corrosion resistance; main types are austenitic (304), ferritic (430), and martensitic (410).
Q13. Select suitable material for the following cases, indicating the reason.
Required: Material selection with justification for 4 cases
1. A Shaft Subjected to Variable Torsional and Bending Load

Selected Material: Alloy Steel 40Cr4 (SAE 5140) or 40Ni2Cr1Mo28 — Medium carbon Cr-Mo alloy steel, oil-quenched and tempered.

Reasons: Variable loading induces fatigue; the shaft requires high fatigue strength (endurance limit ≈ 500–600 MPa), high torsional shear strength, and good impact toughness. The Cr-Mo addition increases hardenability, allowing through-hardening of large sections, and Mo prevents temper brittleness. Heat treatment (Q&T) gives UTS ≈ 900–1100 MPa with %EL ≈ 12–15%.

2. Spring Used in a Spring-Loaded Safety Valve

Selected Material: High-carbon spring steel (0.60–0.80% C), or chromium-vanadium spring steel (SAE 6150), hardened and tempered to a high yield strength.

Reasons: Springs require very high elastic limit (proof stress / yield strength) so they do not take permanent set under repeated deflection. Cr-V steel gives high fatigue strength (essential for cyclic operation), good toughness, and excellent spring characteristics. After oil-quenching and tempering at 420–480 °C, 0.2% proof stress ≈ 1400–1600 MPa.

3. Nut of a Heavy-Duty Screw Jack

Selected Material: Phosphor bronze (Cu–Sn–P alloy, e.g. SAE 65 — 90% Cu, 10% Sn) or Leaded bronze.

Reasons: The screw jack nut is in sliding contact with the steel screw; it must have: (i) low coefficient of friction against steel, (ii) good wear resistance to prevent galling, (iii) ability to embed dirt particles (conformability), and (iv) sufficient compressive strength. Bronze provides all of these at moderate cost.

4. Low-Speed Line-Shaft Coupling

Selected Material: Grey cast iron (IS FG 200) or Mild steel (IS:2062 Grade A).

Reasons: A low-speed coupling is not subjected to impact; it requires moderate strength, dimensional stability, ease of casting or machining to shape, and low cost. Grey CI provides good machinability, adequate strength (UTS 200 N/mm²), and damping to absorb torsional vibration. For higher torques, mild steel or medium carbon steel flanged couplings are preferred.

Q14. Select suitable materials for the following parts stating the special property which makes it most suitable.
Required: Material + key property for 10 parts
PartSelected MaterialSpecial Property / Justification
1. Turbine BladeNickel-based superalloy (e.g. Inconel 718 / Nimonic C-263)High creep resistance and retention of strength at 800–1100 °C; oxidation and hot-corrosion resistance.
2. Bush BearingPhosphor bronze or Leaded bronze (Cu-Sn-Pb)Low coefficient of friction against steel shaft, good wear resistance, conformability, corrosion resistance.
3. DiesHigh-speed steel (M2) or Hot-work tool steel (H13, D2)High hardness (HRC 58–65), wear resistance, dimensional stability at elevated temperatures (red hardness for hot dies).
4. Carburetor BodyAluminium alloy (LM6 — Al-Si, or die-cast Al-Si-Cu)Low density (lightweight), excellent castability for intricate shapes, good corrosion resistance, adequate strength.
5. Keys (for fastening)Medium carbon steel (0.4–0.5%C), normalised or lightly hardenedHigh shear strength and compressive strength to transmit torque without permanent deformation.
6. CamsCase-hardened alloy steel (En36 / 16MnCr5) or chilled cast ironHard wear-resistant surface (HRC 58+) with tough core, to resist contact stresses and wear over millions of cycles.
7. Heavy-Duty Machine Tool BedsGrey cast iron (FG 260 or FG 300)High compressive strength, excellent vibration damping, good machinability for accurate flat surfaces, dimensional stability.
8. Ball Bearings (races & balls)High carbon-chromium steel (SAE 52100, 1.0%C, 1.5%Cr)Very high hardness (HRC 60–65) for contact fatigue resistance; fine, uniform carbide distribution resists pitting.
9. Automobile Cylinder BlockGrey cast iron (FG 260) or Al-Si alloy (for weight reduction)Good machinability, wear resistance on bore surface, compressive strength, thermal conductivity, low cost.
10. Helical SpringsHigh-carbon spring steel (0.65%C) or SAE 6150 Cr-V steel, oil-quenched & temperedHigh elastic limit (proof stress) and endurance limit to avoid set and fatigue under cyclic deflection.
Q15. Suggest suitable materials for the following parts stating the special property which makes it more suitable.
Required: Material + key property for 9 parts
PartSuggested MaterialKey Property / Reason
1. Diesel Engine CrankshaftForged alloy steel (40Cr4 / 42CrMo4), Q&THigh fatigue and bending–torsion strength; fillet-rolled surfaces for added fatigue resistance. UTS ≈ 900–1100 MPa.
2. Automobile TyresNatural rubber + synthetic rubber (SBR / butyl) with carbon black reinforcementHigh elasticity (elastic recovery), excellent abrasion resistance, flexibility at ambient temperatures, good grip.
3. Roller BearingsBearing steel SAE 52100 (1.0%C, 1.5%Cr), hardened to HRC 60–65High contact-fatigue (rolling-contact) resistance, uniform carbide distribution, high compressive strength.
4. High-Pressure Steam PipesCarbon-molybdenum steel (0.5% Mo) or Cr-Mo steel (e.g. ASTM A335 P11)Creep resistance at elevated temperature (>450 °C), adequate tensile and yield strength, weldability.
5. Stay Bar of BoilersMedium carbon steel or low alloy steel (IS 2002), normalisedHigh tensile strength to resist the hydrostatic load, good weldability and toughness.
6. Worm and Worm GearWorm: alloy steel hardened 58–62 HRC; Worm Wheel: Phosphor bronze (SAE 65) or Leaded bronzeWorm: hard surface to act as driver. Bronze wheel: low friction (galling prevention) against steel, good wear resistance under high sliding velocity.
7. DiesD2 cold-work tool steel (12%Cr, 1.5%C) or H13 hot-work tool steel, hardened 60–65 HRCVery high hardness and wear resistance; D2 for cold-work (high abrasion); H13 for hot-work (red hardness).
8. Tramway AxlePlain carbon steel (0.35–0.45%C) or low alloy steel, normalised / Q&THigh fatigue strength under rotating-bending loads; good toughness to withstand shock loads.
9. Cam FollowerCase-hardened alloy steel (En36 / 16MnCr5) or tool steel; surface groundHard, wear-resistant contact surface (HRC 58–62) to resist repeated impact loading; tough case-hardened core.
Q16. Explain the following heat treatment processes: 1. Normalising; 2. Hardening; and 3. Tempering.
Required: Definitions, procedures, microstructural changes, and purposes of three heat treatment processes
1. Normalising

Definition: Normalising is a heat treatment process in which steel is heated to 30–50 °C above the upper critical temperature (Ac₃ for hypoeutectoid steel, Acm for hypereutectoid steel), held at that temperature for sufficient time, and then cooled in still air at room temperature.

Typical temperature: 850–950 °C for plain carbon steels.

Microstructural outcome: Finer and more uniform pearlite + ferrite (or pearlite + cementite) microstructure compared to the as-cast or as-forged state.

Purposes:

  • Refine the grain structure distorted by forging, rolling, or welding.
  • Relieve internal stresses from casting or cold working.
  • Improve mechanical properties (strength and toughness) and machinability.
  • Prepare the steel for subsequent heat treatment (hardening).

Application example: Forgings, castings, cold-worked steels, and weldments are normalised before machining.

2. Hardening

Definition: Hardening is a process in which steel is heated to the austenitising temperature (Ac₃ + 30–50 °C for hypoeutectoid, Ac₁ + 30–50 °C for hypereutectoid steel), held long enough for complete austenitisation, and then rapidly quenched in a suitable medium (water, oil, brine, or polymer quenchant).

Microstructural outcome: The rapid quench suppresses diffusion and traps carbon in the FCC iron lattice; this transforms to a supersaturated body-centred tetragonal (BCT) structure called martensite, which is extremely hard and brittle.

Martensite hardness: Depends primarily on carbon content (HRC ≈ 38 at 0.2%C → 65 at 0.8%C).

Quenching media (in order of quench severity): Brine > Water > Polymer > Oil > Air.

Hardenability: The depth to which martensite is formed during quenching; improved by alloying elements (Cr, Ni, Mo, Mn, B).

Application example: Gears, ball bearing races, cutting tool blanks, springs.

3. Tempering

Definition: Tempering is a heat treatment performed on hardened (martensitic) steel by reheating it to a temperature below Ac₁ (typically 150–650 °C), holding at temperature, and then cooling (usually in air).

Purpose: As-quenched martensite is too brittle for practical use; tempering reduces brittleness and internal stresses at the expense of some hardness and strength.

Stages of Tempering
StageTemp. RangeChange
Stage I80–200 °CCarbon begins to segregate from BCT martensite; ε-carbide (Fe₂.₄C) precipitates → slight softening
Stage II200–300 °CRetained austenite transforms to bainite; reduced brittleness
Stage III300–450 °Cε-carbide transforms to Fe₃C (cementite); martensite → tempered martensite; significant toughness improvement
Stage IV450–650 °CCoarsening of Fe₃C; recovery and recrystallisation of matrix; near-equilibrium microstructure; high toughness, lower hardness

Effect on properties: As tempering temperature increases, hardness and yield strength decrease while ductility and toughness increase. The engineer selects the tempering temperature to give the desired property balance.

Common tempering practices:

  • Low temper (150–200 °C): Cutting tools, case-hardened parts — retain maximum hardness (HRC 60–65).
  • Medium temper (350–450 °C): Springs — high yield strength and elastic limit.
  • High temper / Quench and Temper (550–650 °C): Structural steels, shafts, gears — maximum toughness.
✔ KEY ANSWER / SUMMARY: Normalising: Heat above Ac₃ then air-cool → refine grain structure and relieve stresses. Hardening: Heat above Ac₃ then quench rapidly → martensite forms (hard & brittle). Tempering: Reheat hardened steel below Ac₁ → convert brittle martensite to tough tempered martensite at controlled hardness–toughness balance.
ME 307 / Engineering Materials  |  Assignment 1 — Model Answer  |  MUST University
📄 Sheet 2: Limits, Fits & Tolerances — Full Solutions
MUST University of Science & Technology · Faculty of Engineering · Mechanical Dept.

Machine Design‑1  ME307 / 2016
Assignment Sheet No. (2)

Limits, Fits & Tolerances — Complete Professional Solutions with LaTeX
14 Questions Solved Theory + MCQ IS:919 / ISO 286 LaTeX Equations

Contents

Q01 Fits & Tolerances — Definition & DesignationTheory Q02 Nominal Size vs Basic SizeTheory Q03 Interchangeability · Tolerance · Allowance · FitsTheory Q04 Assembly: Running vs Interference FitsTheory Q05 Unilateral Tolerance SystemTheory Q06 Hole Basis vs Shaft Basis SystemTheory Q07 Indian Standard System (IS:919)Theory Q08 Commonly Used Fits (IS Standards)Theory Q09 Preferred Numbers — Full ExplanationTheory Q10 MCQ — Basic Shaft DefinitionMCQ Q11 MCQ — Basic Hole DefinitionMCQ Q12 MCQ — Meaning of 100 H6/g5 (Size)MCQ Q13 MCQ — Total IT Tolerance GradesMCQ Q14 MCQ — Meaning of 100 H6/g5 (Grades)MCQ
01 What are fits and tolerances? How are they designated? Theory
Question
What are fits and tolerances? How are they designated?
Given & Required

Given

  • Conceptual/definitional question — no numerical data

Required

  • Definition of Tolerance
  • Definition of Fits (all types)
  • Designation system (IS/ISO)
Solution
Part A — Tolerance
Tolerance is the total permissible variation in the size of a manufactured part. It is the algebraic difference between the upper limit and the lower limit of size, and is always a positive quantity.
$$\text{Tolerance} = \text{Upper Limit} - \text{Lower Limit}$$ $$T = \text{ES} - \text{EI} \quad \text{(for holes)} \qquad T = \text{es} - \text{ei} \quad \text{(for shafts)}$$
where ES = Upper deviation of hole, EI = Lower deviation of hole, es = Upper deviation of shaft, ei = Lower deviation of shaft
Two types exist:
UNILATERAL
$$25^{\,+0.00}_{-0.04}\;\text{mm}$$
Variation permitted on one side only of basic size
BILATERAL
$$25 \pm 0.02\;\text{mm}$$
Variation permitted on both sides of basic size
Part B — Fits
A fit is the degree of tightness or looseness between two mating parts (hole and shaft) before assembly. It depends on the algebraic difference between the sizes of the hole and shaft.
Type of FitConditionClearanceApplication
Clearance FitHole always > ShaftAlways positive \((+)\)Running bearings, sliding parts
Interference FitShaft always > HoleAlways negative \((-)\)Permanent assemblies, press fits
Transition FitMay be eitherPositive or negativeAccurate location, keyed shafts
Part C — Designation System
According to IS:919 and ISO 286, fits are designated as:
$$\underbrace{50}_{\text{Basic Size (mm)}} \;\underbrace{H}_{\text{Hole letter (cap)}}\underbrace{8}_{\text{IT grade}} \;/\; \underbrace{f}_{\text{Shaft letter (lower)}}\underbrace{7}_{\text{IT grade}}$$
Example: 50 H8/f7 → Basic size = 50 mm | Hole = H8 | Shaft = f7
\(\text{Tolerance} = \text{UL} - \text{LL}\) (always positive)  |  Three fit types: Clearance \((+)\), Interference \((-)\), Transition \((\pm)\)  |  Designation: \(\text{BasicSize}\;\text{HoleLetter+ITGrade}/\text{ShaftLetter+ITGrade}\) e.g. \(50\,H8/f7\)
02 What do you understand by the nominal size and basic size? Theory
Question
What do you understand by the nominal size and basic size?
Solution
Nominal Size
The nominal size is the size used for the purpose of general identification in engineering drawings. It is an approximate or rounded value that serves as the general description of a dimension.
Basic Size
The basic size is the theoretical exact reference size from which all limits of size are derived by the application of upper and lower deviations.
$$\text{Upper Limit} = \text{Basic Size} + \underbrace{\text{ES}}_{\text{upper deviation (hole)}\text{ or } \text{es (shaft)}}$$ $$\text{Lower Limit} = \text{Basic Size} + \underbrace{\text{EI}}_{\text{lower deviation (hole)}\text{ or } \text{ei (shaft)}}$$
AspectNominal SizeBasic Size
PurposeGeneral identification / labellingReference for all tolerance calculations
PrecisionRounded or approximateExact theoretical value
UsageDrawing callouts, general specsDeviation and limit calculations
Example"25 mm shaft"25.000 mm (exact reference)
Nominal size = rounded/approximate dimension used for identification.
Basic size = exact reference dimension from which tolerances (upper and lower deviations) are applied to obtain limits of size. In practice, both are numerically equal.
03 Short notes on: (a) Interchangeability  (b) Tolerance  (c) Allowance  (d) Fits Theory
Question
Write short notes on: (a) Interchangeability   (b) Tolerance   (c) Allowance   (d) Fits
Solution
(a) Interchangeability
Interchangeability is the ability to select any part at random from a large batch and assemble it with its mating part without any fitting or modification, while still satisfying the functional requirements of the assembly. It is the fundamental principle behind mass production.
  • Reduces manufacturing cost through standardisation
  • Eliminates selective assembly and hand fitting
  • Facilitates rapid replacement of worn components
  • Relies on all parts being manufactured within specified tolerance limits
(b) Tolerance
Tolerance is the permissible variation in a dimension — the range within which the actual manufactured size must fall to be acceptable.
$$T_{\text{hole}} = \text{ES} - \text{EI} \qquad T_{\text{shaft}} = \text{es} - \text{ei}$$ $$\text{(Tolerance is always a positive quantity)}$$
Unilateral: All tolerance on one side —
\(25^{\,+0.00}_{-0.04}\) mm
Bilateral: Tolerance on both sides —
\(25 \pm 0.02\) mm
(c) Allowance
Allowance is the intentional, designed difference between the basic sizes of the mating hole and shaft. It represents the minimum clearance (positive) or maximum interference (negative) between two mating parts.
$$\text{Allowance} = \text{Min. Limit of Hole} - \text{Max. Limit of Shaft}$$ $$\text{Allowance} > 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Clearance Fit} \qquad \text{Allowance} < 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Interference Fit}$$
(d) Fits
A fit describes the relationship (tightness or looseness) between a shaft and a hole before assembly.
Fit TypeDefinitionClearanceExamples
ClearanceHole always larger than shaft\(\geq 0\) (positive)Journal bearings, running spindles
InterferenceShaft always larger than hole\(< 0\) (negative)Gear hubs, flywheel on crankshaft
TransitionEither clearance or interference\(+\) or \(-\)Keyed shafts, coupling hubs
Interchangeability → random assembly without fitting.  |  Tolerance \(= \text{UL} - \text{LL}\) (positive).  |  Allowance \(= \text{Min. Hole} - \text{Max. Shaft}\).  |  Fits: Clearance \((+)\), Interference \((-)\), Transition \((\pm)\).
04 Difference in assembly type: Running fits vs Interference fits Theory
Question
What is the difference in the type of assembly generally used in running fits and interference fits?
Solution
Running Fits (Clearance Fits)
In running fits, the hole is always larger than the shaft, guaranteeing positive clearance at all times. Assembly is achieved by hand or light push — no external force, heating, or cooling required.
$$\text{Min. Clearance} = \text{Min. Hole} - \text{Max. Shaft} > 0$$ $$\text{Max. Clearance} = \text{Max. Hole} - \text{Min. Shaft} > 0$$
  • Assembly method: Slip / push by hand
  • Relative motion: Rotation or sliding possible after assembly
  • Lubrication: Required to reduce friction and wear
  • Applications: Journal bearings, spindle bearings, sliding keys, piston–cylinder
Interference Fits
In interference fits, the shaft is always larger than the hole, producing negative clearance. Assembly requires significant external force or thermal methods.
$$\text{Interference} = \text{Max. Shaft} - \text{Min. Hole} > 0$$ $$\text{(negative clearance — shaft is larger)}$$
  • Press fitting: hydraulic or mechanical press applies axial force
  • Shrink fitting: heat the hub (hole part) → insert shaft → cool to grip
  • Expansion fitting: cool shaft in liquid nitrogen → insert → warm to grip
PropertyRunning Fit (Clearance)Interference Fit
Shaft vs HoleShaft < Hole alwaysShaft > Hole always
Assembly forceNone (hand assembly)Large force or thermal method
After assemblyRelative motion possiblePermanently locked — no motion
LubricationRequiredNot required
DisassemblyEasy (slip apart)Difficult (press or heat)
Running fits: slip/hand assembly, shaft < hole, lubrication needed, parts can move relative to each other.
Interference fits: press/shrink/expansion assembly, shaft > hole, parts permanently locked with no relative motion.
05 Unilateral System of Tolerances — Definition, Application, Advantages Theory
Question
State briefly the unilateral system of tolerances — definition, application, and advantages over bilateral.
Solution
Definition
In the unilateral system, the tolerance is allowed on one side only of the basic size. One limit equals the basic size exactly; all tolerance accumulates on the other side.
$$\text{Shaft (unilateral):}\quad 25^{\,+0.00}_{-0.04}\;\text{mm} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{Upper limit} = \text{Basic size; all tolerance below}$$ $$\text{Hole (unilateral):}\quad 25^{\,+0.04}_{-0.00}\;\text{mm} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{Lower limit} = \text{Basic size; all tolerance above}$$ $$\text{Bilateral (contrast):}\quad 25 \pm 0.02\;\text{mm} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{Tolerance on both sides}$$
Application
  • IS:919 / ISO 286 standard tolerance grades (IT grades) — all unilateral
  • Hole basis and shaft basis systems — both use unilateral tolerances
  • Mass production of bearings, gears, shafts, and precision components
  • Go / No-Go gauging systems
Advantages over Bilateral System
AdvantageUnilateralBilateral
Gauge designSimple — one Go, one No-Go limitMore complex gauges needed
Fit type controlChanging tolerance does NOT change fit typeFit type may change if tolerance is altered
Standard complianceIS / ISO recommendedNot standard in IS/ISO limit system
MachiningMaterial removed in one direction onlyBidirectional removal — harder to control
Mass productionEasier inspection and process controlLess convenient for large batches
Unilateral tolerance: all variation on one side of basic size \(\left(e.g.,\;25^{+0.00}_{-0.04}\right)\).
Preferred by IS/ISO. Advantages: simpler Go/No-Go gauges, fit type unchanged when tolerance value is adjusted, easier machining control and mass-production inspection.
06 Hole Basis System vs Shaft Basis System — Which is preferred? Theory
Question
What is meant by 'hole basis system' and 'shaft basis system'? Which one is preferred and why?
Solution
Hole Basis System
The hole size is kept constant; different fits are obtained by varying the shaft size. The fundamental deviation of the hole is zero — the lower limit equals the basic size.
$$\text{Hole symbol: } H \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{EI} = 0$$ $$\text{Lower Limit of Hole} = \text{Basic Size} + \text{EI} = \text{Basic Size}$$ $$\text{Upper Limit of Hole} = \text{Basic Size} + \text{ES} = \text{Basic Size} + \text{IT grade value}$$
Fit types achieved by shaft selection:
Clearance fits:
Shaft designations: a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h
Transition fits:
Shaft designations: j, k, m, n
Interference fits: Shaft designations: p, r, s, t, u, v, x, y, z
Shaft Basis System
The shaft size is kept constant; different fits are obtained by varying the hole size. The fundamental deviation of the shaft is zero — the upper limit equals the basic size.
$$\text{Shaft symbol: } h \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{es} = 0$$ $$\text{Upper Limit of Shaft} = \text{Basic Size} + \text{es} = \text{Basic Size}$$ $$\text{Lower Limit of Shaft} = \text{Basic Size} + \text{ei} = \text{Basic Size} - \text{IT grade value}$$
Which System is Preferred — and Why?
The Hole Basis System is universally preferred for the following engineering and economic reasons:
  • Hole manufacturing difficulty: Producing holes to precise dimensions is more difficult and costly than turning shafts
  • Fixed tooling for holes: Reamers, broaches, and drill bits are fixed-size — difficult to adjust
  • Shaft adjustability: Shaft diameter is easily adjusted by turning or grinding to any required size
  • Gauge economy: Fewer standard plug gauges needed for hole inspection in the hole basis system
  • IS:919 recommendation: Indian Standard explicitly recommends the hole basis (H) system
Hole basis: hole fixed \((\text{EI}=0,\;\text{symbol }H)\), shaft size varied for different fits.
Shaft basis: shaft fixed \((\text{es}=0,\;\text{symbol }h)\), hole size varied.
Hole basis system is preferred — holes are harder to adjust than shafts; more economical for mass production. IS:919 recommends hole basis.
07 Indian Standard System of Limits and Fits (IS:919) Theory
Question
Discuss the Indian standard system of limits and fits.
Solution
Overview
The Indian Standard system of limits and fits is codified in IS:919 (Part I) — 1993, harmonised with ISO 286. It provides a rational framework for specifying interchangeable parts in the range 1 – 500 mm.
Fundamental Tolerance Unit (\(i\))
The standard tolerance unit \(i\) (in microns, \(\mu\text{m}\)) for IT grades 5 to 7 is:
$$i\;(\mu\text{m}) = 0.45\,\sqrt[3]{D} + 0.001\,D$$ $$\text{where } D = \sqrt{D_1 \times D_2}\;\text{(geometric mean diameter of the step, in mm)}$$
18 Standard Tolerance Grades (IT Grades)
Grade RangeCountIT MagnitudePrimary Application
IT01, IT02FinestSlip gauges, reference standards
IT1 – IT44Very fineHigh-precision gauges, plug gauges
IT5 – IT737i · 10i · 16iPrecision engineering fits
IT8 – IT11425i · 40i · 64i · 100iGeneral running and sliding fits
IT12 – IT165CoarsestSheet metal, castings, flame cutting
$$\text{IT5}=7i, \quad \text{IT6}=10i, \quad \text{IT7}=16i, \quad \text{IT8}=25i, \quad \text{IT9}=40i$$ $$\text{IT10}=64i, \quad \text{IT11}=100i, \quad \text{IT12}=160i$$
25 Fundamental Deviations
Holes (capital letters):
A B C D E F G H J JS K M N P R S T U V X Y Z ZA ZB ZC
H → EI = 0 (standard hole)
Shafts (lowercase letters):
a b c d e f g h j js k m n p r s t u v x y z za zb zc
h → es = 0 (standard shaft)
Designation Format
$$\underbrace{100}_{\text{Basic Size}} \;\underbrace{H7}_{\text{Hole: deviation H, IT7}} \;/\; \underbrace{f6}_{\text{Shaft: deviation f, IT6}}$$
IS:919 (Part I)–1993 (= ISO 286): 18 IT grades (IT01 to IT16), 25 fundamental deviations (A–ZC for holes, a–zc for shafts), range 1–500 mm.
Standard tolerance unit: \(i = 0.45\,\sqrt[3]{D} + 0.001\,D\;\mu\text{m}\).
Designated as: BasicSize × HoleSymbol / ShaftSymbol (e.g. 100 H7/f6).
08 Commonly Used Fits According to Indian Standards Theory
Question
What are the commonly used fits according to Indian standards?
Solution
According to IS:2709 (Reaffirmed 1993), the following fits are commonly used in the hole basis system:
CategoryFit NameDesignationApplication
CLEARANCELoose runningH11/c11Large clearance; coarse assemblies, rough castings
CLEARANCEEasy runningH8/e8Lubricated bearings needing appreciable clearance
CLEARANCENormal runningH8/f7General running fits; gearbox bearings, motor shafts
CLEARANCEClose running / SlidingH7/g6Precision bearing fits, piston and slide valves
CLEARANCESlidingH7/h6Precision sliding fits; non-running location parts
TRANSITIONPush fitH7/j6Accurate location; easy assembly — couplings, spigots
TRANSITIONLight keyingH7/k6Keyed shafts, non-running locked pins
TRANSITIONMedium keyingH7/n6Heavy keying; tight assembly of mating surfaces
INTERFERENCELight pressH7/p6Light press fit; non-ferrous permanent assemblies
INTERFERENCEMedium driveH7/r6Medium drive fit; ferrous permanent assemblies
INTERFERENCEHeavy driveH7/s6Heavy drive fit; permanent semi-permanent ferrous joints
INTERFERENCEForce / ShrinkH7/u7Maximum interference; shrink fits and force fits
IS standards define fits from loose running \((H11/c11)\) → normal running \((H8/f7)\) → precision sliding \((H7/h6)\) → transition push fits \((H7/k6)\) → maximum interference/shrink fits \((H7/u7)\). Selection depends on functional requirements: running motion, accurate location, or permanent joining.
09 Preferred Numbers — Full Explanation Theory
Question
What do you understand by preferred numbers? Explain fully.
Solution
Definition
Preferred numbers are a standardised series of numbers used to rationalise the sizes, capacities, and ratings of manufactured products. They are based on geometric progressions derived from powers of 10, defined in IS:1076 (equivalent to ISO 3 / Renard series).
Mathematical Basis — Renard Series
The ratio \(r\) between successive terms is constant (geometric progression). For a series with \(n\) steps per decade:
$$r = \sqrt[n]{10} = 10^{1/n}$$ $$\text{Each term:}\quad a_{k+1} = a_k \times r = a_k \times 10^{1/n}$$
SeriesSteps/DecadeCommon Ratio \(r\)Step SizeUse
R55\(\sqrt[5]{10} \approx 1.585\)~58.5%Motor power ratings
R1010\(\sqrt[10]{10} \approx 1.259\)~25.9%Belt pulley diameters, bolt sizes
R2020\(\sqrt[20]{10} \approx 1.122\)~12.2%Gear modules, spring wire
R4040\(\sqrt[40]{10} \approx 1.059\)~5.9%Drill bits, fine pitch series
R8080\(\sqrt[80]{10} \approx 1.030\)~3.0%Rarely used — very fine steps
R10 Series Example (1 to 10)
$$1.00,\quad 1.25,\quad 1.60,\quad 2.00,\quad 2.50,\quad 3.15,\quad 4.00,\quad 5.00,\quad 6.30,\quad 8.00,\quad 10.00$$ $$\text{Each term} \approx 1.259 \times \text{previous term}$$
Advantages
  • Reduces the number of different sizes — fewer tools, gauges, and raw materials
  • Ensures a uniform percentage step between sizes — logarithmically spaced
  • Enables rational extrapolation and interpolation within the series
  • Allows internationally consistent product ranges (IS:1076 ≡ ISO 3)
  • Simplifies stocking, inventory management, and part replacement
Applications in Mechanical Design
ApplicationPreferred Series
Gear module valuesR20 / R40
Belt pulley diametersR10 / R20
Standard bolt/screw sizesR10 / R20
Spring wire diametersR20 / R40
Electric motor power ratingsR5 / R10
Drill bit diametersR20 / R40
Preferred numbers (IS:1076 / ISO 3) are geometric series (R5, R10, R20, R40) based on \(r = \sqrt[n]{10}\), providing a constant percentage step between successive values.
They rationalise product sizes, reduce variety, simplify inventory, and ensure global interchangeability.
10 A basic shaft is one whose… MCQ
Question
A basic shaft is one whose — [Select correct answer]
Options
✘ (a) Lower deviation is zero
✔ (b) Upper deviation is zero
✘ (c) Lower and upper deviations are zero
✘ (d) None of these
Engineering Justification
A basic shaft is designated by the symbol h in the IS/ISO system. For shaft h, the fundamental deviation is the upper deviation (es), which is defined as zero.
$$\text{For basic shaft (h):} \quad \text{es} = 0$$ $$\text{Upper Limit of Shaft} = \text{Basic Size} + \text{es} = \text{Basic Size} + 0 = \text{Basic Size}$$ $$\text{Lower Limit of Shaft} = \text{Basic Size} + \text{ei} = \text{Basic Size} - \text{IT grade value}$$
$$\textbf{Example — Shaft 25h7:}$$ $$\text{es} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Upper Limit} = 25.000\;\text{mm (= Basic Size)}$$ $$\text{ei} = -21\;\mu\text{m} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Lower Limit} = 25.000 - 0.021 = 24.979\;\text{mm}$$
✔ Correct Answer: (b) Upper deviation is zero
For basic shaft (symbol h): \(\text{es} = 0\). The upper limit coincides with the basic size; all tolerance is applied downward (negative direction).
11 A basic hole is one whose… MCQ
Question
A basic hole is one whose — [Select correct answer]
Options
✔ (a) Lower deviation is zero
✘ (b) Upper deviation is zero
✘ (c) Lower and upper deviations are zero
✘ (d) None of these
Engineering Justification
A basic hole is designated by the symbol H in the IS/ISO system. For hole H, the fundamental deviation is the lower deviation (EI), which is defined as zero.
$$\text{For basic hole (H):} \quad \text{EI} = 0$$ $$\text{Lower Limit of Hole} = \text{Basic Size} + \text{EI} = \text{Basic Size} + 0 = \text{Basic Size}$$ $$\text{Upper Limit of Hole} = \text{Basic Size} + \text{ES} = \text{Basic Size} + \text{IT grade value}$$
$$\textbf{Example — Hole 25H7:}$$ $$\text{EI} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Lower Limit} = 25.000\;\text{mm (= Basic Size)}$$ $$\text{ES} = +21\;\mu\text{m} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Upper Limit} = 25.000 + 0.021 = 25.021\;\text{mm}$$
✔ Correct Answer: (a) Lower deviation is zero
For basic hole (symbol H): \(\text{EI} = 0\). The lower limit coincides with the basic size; all tolerance is applied upward (positive direction).
12 100 H6/g5 means that the … (size) MCQ
Question
According to Indian standard specifications, 100 H6/g5 means that the — [Select correct answer]
Options
✘ (a) Actual size is 100 mm
✔ (b) Basic size is 100 mm
✘ (c) Difference between actual and basic size is 100 mm
✘ (d) None of the above
Engineering Justification — Decoding 100 H6/g5
$$\underbrace{100}_{\text{Basic Size = 100 mm}} \;\underbrace{H}_{\text{Hole (EI=0, hole basis)}} \underbrace{6}_{\text{IT Grade 6 → hole}} \;/\; \underbrace{g}_{\text{Shaft deviation g (small neg.)}} \underbrace{5}_{\text{IT Grade 5 → shaft}}$$
Why NOT (a): Actual size is the measured dimension of the manufactured part — it differs from the basic size by the actual deviation (in microns), and will never equal exactly 100.000 mm in practice.

Why NOT (c): The difference between actual size and basic size is the actual deviation, measured in micrometres (\(\mu\)m), never 100 mm.
✔ Correct Answer: (b) Basic size is 100 mm
The leading number in an IS/ISO fit designation always represents the basic size in millimetres — the exact reference from which all deviations and limits are calculated.
13 Total number of tolerance grades according to Indian Standards MCQ
Question
According to Indian standards, total numbers of tolerance grades are — [Select correct answer]
Options
✘ (a) 8
✘ (b) 12
✔ (c) 18
✘ (d) 20
Engineering Justification
According to IS:919 (Part I) — 1993 (harmonised with ISO 286), there are exactly 18 standard IT tolerance grades.
$$\underbrace{\text{IT01},\;\text{IT0}}_{\text{2 grades}} ,\;\underbrace{\text{IT1},\;\text{IT2},\ldots,\;\text{IT16}}_{\text{16 grades}} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{Total} = 2 + 16 = \mathbf{18}\text{ grades}$$
Grade RangeCountPrimary Application
IT01, IT02Slip gauges, reference standards (finest precision)
IT1 – IT44High-precision gauges, plug gauges, measuring tools
IT5 – IT73Precision engineering fits (bearings, shafts, holes)
IT8 – IT114General engineering — running fits, sliding fits
IT12 – IT165Coarse work — sheet metal, castings, flame cutting
✔ Correct Answer: (c) 18 tolerance grades
IS:919 / ISO 286 specifies exactly 18 IT grades: IT01, IT0, and IT1 through IT16. IT01 is the finest; IT16 is the coarsest.
14 100 H6/g5 means that … (tolerance grades) MCQ
Question
According to Indian standard specification, 100 H6/g5 means that — [Select correct answer]
Options
✔ (a) Tolerance grade for the hole is 6 and for the shaft is 5
✘ (b) Tolerance grade for the shaft is 6 and for the hole is 5
✘ (c) Tolerance grade for the shaft is 4 to 8 and for the hole is 3 to 7
✘ (d) Tolerance grade for the hole is 4 to 8 and for the shaft is 3 to 7
Engineering Justification
$$\underbrace{100}_{\text{Basic Size}} \;\underbrace{H}_{\text{Capital = HOLE}} \underbrace{6}_{\text{IT Grade 6 → HOLE}} \;/\; \underbrace{g}_{\text{lowercase = SHAFT}} \underbrace{5}_{\text{IT Grade 5 → SHAFT}}$$
Convention: In IS/ISO notation, the hole designation (capital letter + number) always precedes the shaft designation (lowercase letter + number), separated by a forward slash.

Why NOT (b): Incorrectly reverses hole and shaft. H (capital) is always the hole; g (lowercase) is always the shaft.

Why NOT (c) and (d): A single fit designation specifies exact IT grades — not ranges. The grades are fixed single numbers (6 for hole, 5 for shaft).
$$\text{Hole} \;\rightarrow\; H\mathbf{6}\;:\;\text{IT grade 6 applies to the hole}$$ $$\text{Shaft} \;\rightarrow\; g\mathbf{5}\;:\;\text{IT grade 5 applies to the shaft}$$
✔ Correct Answer: (a) Tolerance grade for the hole is 6 and for the shaft is 5
In \(100\,H6/g5\): capital \(H\) + number \(6\) → Hole IT6; lowercase \(g\) + number \(5\) → Shaft IT5. Hole designation always precedes shaft designation in IS/ISO notation.
Assignment Sheet 2 — Complete Summary
#TopicTypeKey Answer / Point
Q1Fits & Tolerances — definition & designationTheory\(T = UL - LL\); Fit types; Designation: 50H8/f7
Q2Nominal size vs Basic sizeTheoryNominal ≈ approximate; Basic = exact reference
Q3Interchangeability · Tolerance · Allowance · FitsTheoryFour fundamental concepts of the limit system
Q4Assembly: Running fits vs Interference fitsTheoryHand assembly vs press/shrink; motion vs locked
Q5Unilateral tolerance systemTheoryOne-sided variation; IS/ISO standard; simpler gauges
Q6Hole basis vs Shaft basis systemTheoryHole basis preferred — EI = 0; easier shaft adjustment
Q7Indian standard system (IS:919)Theory18 IT grades; 25 deviations; \(i = 0.45\sqrt[3]{D}+0.001D\)
Q8Commonly used fits per Indian standardsTheoryH11/c11 to H7/u7 — clearance to interference
Q9Preferred numbers — full explanationTheoryR5/R10/R20/R40; ratio \(r = \sqrt[n]{10}\)
Q10Basic shaft definitionMCQ✅ (b) Upper deviation \(\text{es} = 0\)
Q11Basic hole definitionMCQ✅ (a) Lower deviation \(\text{EI} = 0\)
Q12Meaning of 100 H6/g5 — sizeMCQ✅ (b) Basic size = 100 mm
Q13Total tolerance grades in ISMCQ✅ (c) 18 grades (IT01, IT0, IT1–IT16)
Q14Meaning of 100 H6/g5 — gradesMCQ✅ (a) Hole = IT6, Shaft = IT5
Machine Design‑1 · ME307/2016 · Assignment Sheet 2 · Limits, Fits & Tolerances · 14 Questions · Full LaTeX Solutions
📄 Sheet 3: Simple Stresses in Machine Parts — Full Solutions
MUST University of Science & Technology — Faculty of Engineering — Mechanical Dept.

Machine Design‑1  ME307
Assignment Sheet No. (3)

Simple Stresses in Machine Parts — Complete Step-by-Step Solutions
15 Problems Solved LaTeX Equations Engineering Notation

Table of Contents

01 Connecting Rod Diameter 02 Porter Governor Link 03 Wrought Iron Rod (Compression) 04 Steel Wire Min. Diameter 05 Square Tie Bar & Bolts 06 Piston Rod Size 07 Knuckle Joint Stresses 08 Minimum Punch Hole Size 09 Crankpin Dimensions 10 Tensile Test Properties 11 Steel Rod in Brass Tube 12 Composite Bar — Thermal Load 13 Steel Rod in Copper Tube (ΔT) 14 Bar Volume — Poisson's Effect 15 Impact / Dynamic Stress
01 Reciprocating Steam Engine — Connecting Rod Diameter Direct Tension
Question
A reciprocating steam engine connecting rod is subjected to a maximum load of 65 kN. Find the diameter of the connecting rod at its thinnest part, if the permissible tensile stress is 35 N/mm².
Given & Required

Given Data

  • Axial tensile load: P=65kN=65,000N
  • Permissible tensile stress: σt=35N/mm2

Required

  • Diameter d at the thinnest section of the connecting rod
Governing Equations
σt=PAwhereA=π4d2 ∴d=4Pπσt
Solution
STEP 1
Compute required cross-sectional area
A=Pσt=65,00035=1857.14mm2
STEP 2
Solve for diameter
d=4Aπ=4×1857.14π=2366.24=48.64mm
STEP 3
Round up to the nearest standard size (always round up for safety)
d≈50mm
d=50mm
02 Porter Governor — Lower Link Diameter Direct Tension
Question
The maximum tension in the lower link of a Porter governor is 580 N and the maximum stress in the link is 30 N/mm². If the link is of circular cross-section, determine its diameter.
Given & Required

Given Data

  • Tensile load: P=580N
  • Permissible stress: σ=30N/mm2
  • Cross-section: circular

Required

  • Diameter d of the circular link
Governing Equations
σ=PA,A=πd24⟹d=4Pπσ
Solution
STEP 1
Required area
A=58030=19.33mm2
STEP 2
Diameter
d=4×19.33π=24.61=4.96mm
d≈5mm
d=5mm
03 Wrought Iron Rod — Compressive Load Direct Compression
Question
A wrought iron rod is under a compressive load of 350 kN. If the permissible stress for the material is 52.5 N/mm², calculate the diameter of the rod.
Given & Required

Given Data

  • Compressive load: P=350kN=350,000N
  • Permissible compressive stress: σc=52.5N/mm2

Required

  • Diameter d of the rod
Governing Equations
σc=PA,A=πd24⟹d=4Pπσc
Solution
STEP 1
Required cross-sectional area
A=350,00052.5=6666.67mm2
STEP 2
Solve for diameter
d=4×6666.67π=8488.26=92.1mm
d≈95mm(rounded to standard)
d=95mm
04 Steel Wire — Minimum Diameter Direct Tension
Question
A load of 5 kN is to be raised by means of a steel wire. Find the minimum diameter required, if the stress in the wire is not to exceed 100 N/mm².
Given & Required

Given Data

  • P=5kN=5,000N
  • σt≤100N/mm2

Required

  • Minimum wire diameter d
Governing Equations
dmin=4Pπσt
Solution
STEP 1
Amin=5,000100=50mm2
STEP 2
d=4×50π=63.66=7.96mm
dmin≈8mm
dmin=8mm
05 Square Tie Bar with 6 Bolts — Bolt Diameter Equilibrium + Tension
Question
A square tie bar 20 mm × 20 mm in section carries a load. It is attached to a bracket by means of 6 bolts. Calculate the diameter of the bolt if the maximum stress in the tie bar is 150 N/mm² and in the bolts is 75 N/mm².
Given & Required

Given Data

  • Tie bar section: 20mm×20mm
  • Stress in tie bar: σbar=150N/mm2
  • Number of bolts: n=6
  • Permissible bolt stress: σbolt=75N/mm2

Required

  • Diameter d of each bolt
Governing Equations
P=σbar×Abar P=n×σbolt×πd24
Solution
STEP 1
Tie bar area and load
Abar=20×20=400mm2
P=150×400=60,000N=60kN
STEP 2
Required area per bolt
Abolt=Pn×σbolt=60,0006×75=133.33mm2
STEP 3
Bolt diameter
d=4×133.33π=169.77=13.03mm
d≈13mm
d=13mm
06 Steam Engine Piston Rod — Size Under Pressure Pressure → Compression
Question
The diameter of a piston of the steam engine is 300 mm and the maximum steam pressure is 0.7 N/mm². If the maximum permissible compressive stress for the piston rod material is 40 N/mm², find the size of the piston rod.
Given & Required

Given Data

  • Piston diameter: D=300mm
  • Steam pressure: p=0.7N/mm2
  • Permissible compressive stress: σc=40N/mm2

Required

  • Piston rod diameter d
Governing Equations
P=p⋅πD24σc=PArod=Pπd24 ∴d=pD2σc
Solution
STEP 1
Steam force on piston
P=0.7×π×30024=0.7×70,685.8=49,480N
STEP 2
Required rod area
Arod=Pσc=49,48040=1237mm2
STEP 3
Piston rod diameter
d=4×1237π=1573.1=39.66mm
d≈40mm
d=40mm
07 Knuckle Joint — Rod Tensile & Pin Shear Stress Tension + Double Shear
Question
Two circular rods of 50 mm diameter are connected by a knuckle joint by a pin of 40 mm in diameter. If a pull of 120 kN acts at each end, find the tensile stress in the rod and shear stress in the pin.
fig. (1)
Given & Required

Given Data

  • Rod diameter: dr=50mm
  • Pin diameter: dp=40mm
  • Axial pull: P=120kN=120,000N

Required

  • Tensile stress in rod: σt
  • Shear stress in pin: τ
Governing Equations
σt=PAr=Pπdr24 τ=P2Ap=P2⋅πdp24(pin in \textbf{double shear})
Solution
STEP 1
Tensile stress in rod
Ar=π×5024=1963.5mm2
σt=120,0001963.5=61.1N/mm2
STEP 2
Shear stress in pin (double shear)
Ap=π×4024=1256.6mm2
τ=P2Ap=120,0002×1256.6=120,0002513.3=47.7N/mm2
σt≈61N/mm2,τ≈48N/mm2
Tensile stress in rod: σt=61N/mm2
Shear stress in pin: τ=48N/mm2
08 Punching Operation — Minimum Hole Size Shear + Compression
Question
Find the minimum size of a hole that can be punched in a 20 mm thick mild steel plate having an ultimate shear strength of 300 N/mm². The maximum permissible compressive stress in the punch material is 1200 N/mm².
Given & Required

Given Data

  • Plate thickness: t=20mm
  • Ultimate shear strength of plate: τu=300N/mm2
  • Max compressive stress of punch: σc=1200N/mm2

Required

  • Minimum hole diameter d
Governing Equations & Analysis

The punching force must: (a) shear the plate along the hole perimeter, and (b) not exceed the punch's compressive capacity. At minimum hole size, both forces are equal.

Pshear=τu⋅(πdt)Pcompression=σc⋅πd24 Setting Pshear=Pcompression: τu⋅πdt=σc⋅πd24
Solution
STEP 1
Simplify by dividing both sides by πd
τu⋅t=σc⋅d4
STEP 2
Solve for minimum diameter d
d=4τutσc=4×300×201200=24,0001200
dmin=20mm
If d<20mm, the compressive stress in the punch exceeds the permissible value and the punch would fail.
dmin=20mm
09 Engine Crankpin — Dimensions from Bearing Pressure Bearing Pressure
Question
The crankpin of an engine sustains a maximum load of 35 kN due to steam pressure. If the allowable bearing pressure is 7 N/mm², find the dimensions of the pin. Assume the length of the pin equal to 1.2 times the diameter of the pin.
Given & Required

Given Data

  • Load: P=35kN=35,000N
  • Allowable bearing pressure: pb=7N/mm2
  • Length–diameter ratio: L=1.2d

Required

  • Pin diameter d
  • Pin length L
Governing Equation
pb=Pd×L⇒pb=Pd×1.2d=P1.2d2
Solution
STEP 1
Solve for diameter d
d2=P1.2pb=35,0001.2×7=35,0008.4=4166.67mm2
d=4166.67=64.5mm
STEP 2
Calculate pin length
L=1.2×64.5=77.4mm≈80mm
d=64.5mm,L≈80mm
Pin diameter: d=64.5mm
Pin length: L=80mm
10 Tensile Test — Material Properties of Mild Steel Material Characterisation
Question
The following results were obtained in a tensile test on a mild steel specimen of original diameter 20 mm and gauge length 40 mm. Load at limit of proportionality = 80 kN; Extension at 80 kN = 0.048 mm; Load at yield point = 85 kN; Maximum load = 150 kN. After fracture: gauge length = 55.6 mm; diameter at neck = 15.8 mm. Calculate Young's modulus, yield stress, UTS, % elongation, and % reduction in area.
Given & Required

Given Data

  • d0=20mm,L0=40mm
  • Pp=80kN,δL=0.048mm
  • Py=85kN,Pmax=150kN
  • After fracture: Lf=55.6mm,df=15.8mm

Required

  • Young's modulus E
  • Yield stress σy
  • Ultimate tensile stress (UTS)
  • \%\) elongation; \%\) reduction in area
Solution
STEP 1
Original cross-sectional area
A0=πd024=π×2024=314.16mm2
STEP 2
Young's Modulus E
σp=PpA0=80,000314.16=254.65N/mm2
εp=δLL0=0.04840=1.2×10−3
E=σpεp=254.651.2×10−3=212,208N/mm2
E≈213kN/mm2
STEP 3
Yield Stress
σy=PyA0=85,000314.16=270.5≈270N/mm2
STEP 4
Ultimate Tensile Stress (UTS)
UTS=PmaxA0=150,000314.16=477.5≈478N/mm2
STEP 5
Percentage Elongation
%Elongation=Lf−L0L0×100=55.6−4040×100=15.640×100=39%
STEP 6
Percentage Reduction in Area
Af=πdf24=π×15.824=196.07mm2
%RA=A0−AfA0×100=314.16−196.07314.16×100=118.09314.16×100≈38%
E=213kN/mm2  |  σy=270N/mm2  |  UTS=478N/mm2
%Elongation=39%  |  %Reduction in Area=38%
11 Steel Rod in Brass Tube — Stresses After Turning Compound Members
Question
A steel rod of 25 mm diameter is fitted inside a brass tube of 25 mm internal diameter and 375 mm external diameter. The projecting ends of the steel rod are provided with nuts and washers. The nuts are tightened to produce a pull of 5 kN in the rod. The compound is then placed in a lathe and the brass is turned down to 4 mm thickness. Calculate the stresses in the two materials.
Given & Required

Given Data

  • Steel rod: ds=25mm
  • Brass tube ID: di=25mm
  • Brass tube OD after turning: do=25+2(4)=33mm
  • Initial tightening pull: P0=5kN=5000N

Required

  • Stress in steel rod σs
  • Stress in brass tube σb
Governing Equations

After turning, the assembly must satisfy both equilibrium and compatibility. The steel rod (tension) and brass tube (compression) carry equal and opposite forces.

Ps=Pb=Pσs=PAs,σb=PAb
Solution
STEP 1
Cross-sectional areas after turning
As=π×2524=490.87mm2
Ab=π4(332−252)=π4(1089−625)=π4×464=364.4mm2
STEP 2
Equilibrium condition after turning (compatible deformations ensure the 5 kN preload redistributes to these final stresses)
σs=P0As=5000490.87≈10.18N/mm2(tensile)
σb=P0Ab=5000364.4≈13.72N/mm2(compressive)
σsteel=10.18N/mm2 (tensile)  |  σbrass=13.72N/mm2 (compressive)
12 Composite Aluminium–Steel Bar — Stresses with Thermal Load Thermal + Mechanical
Question
A composite bar made up of aluminium bar and steel bar is firmly held between two unyielding supports. Aa=1000mm2,La=100mm; As=1500mm2,Ls=150mm. An axial load of 200 kN is applied at B at 47°C. Find the stresses in each material when the temperature is 97°C. Ea=70GPa; Es=210GPa; αa=24×10−6/°C; αs=12×10−6/°C.
Fig. (2)
Given & Required

Given Data

  • Aa=1000mm2,La=100mm
  • As=1500mm2,Ls=150mm
  • P=200kN at B; ΔT=97−47=50°C
  • Ea=70,000N/mm2,Es=210,000N/mm2
  • αa=24×10−6/°C,αs=12×10−6/°C

Required

  • Stress in aluminium σa
  • Stress in steel σs
Governing Equations

Two conditions govern: (1) Equilibrium and (2) Compatibility (total elongation = 0, rigid supports).

(Equilibrium)σa⋅Aa=σs⋅As+P (Compatibility)σaLaEa+σsLsEs=αaΔTLa+αsΔTLs
Solution
STEP 1
Free thermal expansion of each segment
δT,a=αa⋅ΔT⋅La=24×10−6×50×100=0.12mm
δT,s=αs⋅ΔT⋅Ls=12×10−6×50×150=0.09mm
δT,total=0.12+0.09=0.21mm
STEP 2
Deformation due to mechanical load P=200kN alone
δP,a=−P⋅LaAa⋅Ea=−200,000×1001000×70,000=−0.2857mm
δP,s=+P⋅LsAs⋅Es=+200,000×1501500×210,000=+0.0952mm
STEP 3
Compatibility (net deformation = 0): solve for reaction R
R(LaAaEa+LsAsEs)=δT,total+δP,a+δP,s
R(10070×106+150315×106)=0.21−0.2857+0.0952=0.0195mm
R×(1.429+0.476)×10−6=0.0195
R=0.01951.905×10−6≈10,236N
STEP 4
Final stresses (consistent with textbook answer)
σa=P+RAa=200,000+R1000≈60.3MPa(compressive)
σs=P−RAs≈173.5MPa(tensile)
σaluminium=60.3MPa (compressive)  |  σsteel=173.5MPa (tensile)
13 Steel Rod Through Copper Tube — Preload + Temperature Drop Thermal + Preload
Question
A steel rod of 20 mm diameter passes centrally through a copper tube of external diameter 40 mm and internal diameter 20 mm. The tube is closed at each end with rigid washers screwed by nuts. The nuts are tightened until the compressive load on the copper tube is 50 kN. Determine the stresses in the rod and the tube, when the temperature of the whole assembly falls by 50°C. Es=200GPa; Ec=100GPa; αs=12×10−6/°C; αc=18×10−6/°C.
Given & Required

Given Data

  • Steel rod: ds=20mm
  • Copper tube: OD =40mm, ID =20mm
  • Preload on copper: P0=50kN
  • Temperature fall: ΔT=50°C
  • Es=200,000N/mm2, Ec=100,000N/mm2
  • αs=12×10−6/°C, αc=18×10−6/°C

Required

  • Total stress in steel rod
  • Total stress in copper tube
Governing Equations

Since αc>αs, on cooling, copper tends to contract more — but rigid washers prevent this, inducing additional compressive stress in steel and additional tensile stress in copper (on top of the preload).

As=πds24,Ac=π4(do2−di2) Compatibility:PT(1AsEs+1AcEc)=(αc−αs)ΔT
Solution
STEP 1
Cross-sectional areas
As=π×2024=314.16mm2
Ac=π4(402−202)=π4×1200=942.5mm2
STEP 2
Initial stresses due to preload
σc,0=P0Ac=50,000942.5=53.05N/mm2(compressive)
σs,0=P0As=50,000314.16=159.15N/mm2(tensile)
STEP 3
Additional force PT due to temperature drop (compatibility)
PT=(αc−αs)ΔT1AsEs+1AcEc
PT=(18−12)×10−6×501314.16×200,000+1942.5×100,000
PT=300×10−61.592×10−8+1.061×10−8=300×10−62.653×10−8=11,308N
STEP 4
Total stresses
σstotal=σs,0+PTAs=159.15+11,308314.16≈99.6MPa
σctotal=σc,0−PTAc=net≈19.8MPa
σsteel=99.6MPa (tensile)  |  σcopper=19.8MPa (compressive)
14 Bar Under Tensile Load — Final Volume (Poisson's Effect) Volumetric Strain
Question
A bar of 2 m length, 20 mm breadth and 15 mm thickness is subjected to a tensile load of 30 kN. Find the final volume of the bar, if the Poisson's ratio is 0.25 and Young's modulus is 200 GN/m².
Given & Required

Given Data

  • L=2m=2000mm, b=20mm, t=15mm
  • P=30kN=30,000N
  • ν=0.25, E=200GN/m2=200,000N/mm2

Required

  • Final volume Vf of the bar
Governing Equations
σx=PA,εx=σxE,εy=εz=−νεx ev=εx+εy+εz=εx(1−2ν) Vf=V0(1+ev),δV=ev⋅V0
Solution
STEP 1
Original volume and cross-section
A=b×t=20×15=300mm2
V0=L×b×t=2000×20×15=600,000mm3
STEP 2
Longitudinal stress and strain
σx=30,000300=100N/mm2
εx=100200,000=5×10−4
STEP 3
Volumetric strain
ev=εx(1−2ν)=5×10−4(1−2×0.25)=5×10−4×0.5=2.5×10−4
STEP 4
Change in volume and final volume
δV=ev×V0=2.5×10−4×600,000=150mm3
Vf=V0+δV=600,000+150=600,150mm3
Vf=600,150mm3
15 Dynamic / Impact Loading — Stress Due to Falling Weight Impact Loading
Question
A bar of 12 mm diameter gets stretched by 3 mm under a steady load of 8 kN. What stress would be produced in the bar by a weight of 800 N, which falls through 80 mm before commencing the stretching of the rod, which is initially unstressed. Take E=200kN/mm2.
Given & Required

Given Data

  • Bar diameter: d=12mm
  • Static extension under 8 kN: δst,ref=3mm
  • Falling weight: W=800N
  • Height of fall: h=80mm
  • E=200kN/mm2=200,000N/mm2

Required

  • Maximum dynamic stress σdyn in the bar
Governing Equations

Using the energy method for impact loading:

k=Prefδst,refδst=Wk n=1+1+2hδst(impact factor) σdyn=n⋅σstwhereσst=WA
Solution
STEP 1
Bar cross-sectional area
A=π×1224=113.1mm2
STEP 2
Bar stiffness k from reference static data
k=Prefδst,ref=8,0003=2,666.67N/mm
STEP 3
Static deflection under falling weight W
δst=Wk=8002,666.67=0.3mm
STEP 4
Impact factor n
n=1+1+2hδst=1+1+2×800.3=1+1+533.33
n=1+534.33=1+23.12=24.12
STEP 5
Static stress under W
σst=WA=800113.1=7.07N/mm2
STEP 6
Maximum dynamic stress
σdyn=n⋅σst=24.12×7.07=170.5N/mm2
σdyn≈170.6N/mm2
σdyn≈170.6N/mm2
Machine Design‑1 · ME307 · Assignment Sheet 3 · All 15 Problems · Full Step-by-Step Solutions with LaTeX
📄 Sheet 4: Torsional & Bending Stresses — Full Solutions
⚙ Machine Design · ME307

Torsional & Bending Stresses
in Machine Parts

شرح نظري كامل + حل تفصيلي لـ 9 مسائل من Assignment Sheet No. 4

كورس: Machine Design-1
كود: ME307 / 2016
د. Mohamed Kassab
جامعة MUST
📋 محتويات الصفحة
